title: Prompt Engineering at Scale
description: Manage prompts as versioned software artifacts: registries, A/B testing, rollout strategies, automated regression testing, and prompt performance analytics for production LLM systems.
learningObjectives: Design a versioned prompt registry with rollback capability
Implement A/B testing with statistical significance testing
Apply prompt templating with variable injection and validation
Measure prompt performance metrics in production

sections.title: Why Prompt Management Becomes Critical at Scale
sections.content: At prototype stage, prompts live in code comments or sticky notes. At production scale with thousands of daily users, an unversioned prompt change silently degrades output quality, breaks edge cases, or violates safety policies with no rollback path. PromptOps treats prompts as first-class software artifacts: versioned, tested, and deployed with the same rigour as code. Teams that invest in prompt infrastructure reduce regression incidents by 60-80% and accelerate prompt iteration velocity.

sections.title: Prompt Registry, Versioning & A/B Testing
sections.content:
**1. Prompt as Artifact**
A prompt has: `name`, `version` (semver), `template` (Jinja2/f-string), `variables` (typed), `metadata` (author, date, description), `test_cases`.

**2. Versioning Scheme**
- Major: changes expected output structure (breaking)
- Minor: improves quality without structural change
- Patch: typo fixes, minor wording

**3. Prompt Registry**
Central store (YAML/JSON/DB) keyed by `name:version`. Supports:
- `get(name, version='latest')` — resolves alias
- `promote(name, from_version, to_version='production')` — atomic swap
- `rollback(name)` — restore previous production version

**4. A/B Testing Framework**
- Assign users to buckets deterministically: `bucket = hash(user_id + experiment_id) % 100`
- Collect metrics per bucket: task_success, user_rating, cost, latency
- Significance test: two-proportion z-test for binary outcomes, t-test for continuous
- Rollout strategy: 5% → 20% → 50% → 100% gated by metric thresholds

**5. Automated Regression Suite**
For each prompt change, run golden test cases:
- Compare output against reference (ROUGE-L, keyword presence)
- Flag regressions with configurable thresholds
- Gate deployment if regression_rate > 5%

**6. Prompt Analytics**
Track per-version: mean quality score, cost/request, latency, error rate. Dashboard enables data-driven rollback decisions.

sections.title: Prompt Registry, A/B Router & Regression Runner
Common Prompt Management Mistakes
sections.content:
**1. Prompt strings in source code without versioning**
Changing a prompt string inline has no audit trail, no rollback, and no way to correlate quality changes with prompt changes. Always use a registry.

**2. Running A/B tests without statistical significance**
Declaring a winner after 10 samples is noise. Require n≥30 per bucket and |z| > 1.96 before drawing conclusions.

**3. Not testing prompts against adversarial inputs**
Golden cases with clean inputs pass, but production users send misspellings, code, other languages. Include adversarial cases in the regression suite.

**4. Ignoring variable validation**
Missing or extra variables in `template.format(**kwargs)` raise `KeyError` or silently inject unexpected text. Validate at registry registration time.

**5. Treating prompt version as independent from model version**
A prompt optimised for GPT-4 may regress on GPT-4o or Claude. Store model name alongside prompt version; test the combination.

**6. Big-bang prompt rollouts**
Deploying a new prompt to 100% of users immediately has no safety net. Use staged rollout: 5% → 20% → 50% → 100% with automated metric gates.

sections.title: Build a Prompt CI Pipeline
sections.content:
**Task:** Implement a prompt CI pipeline that runs on every prompt change.

**Requirements:**
1. `PromptRegistry` backed by a JSON file (load on start, save on register/promote)
2. `RegressionRunner` with at least 5 golden cases per prompt
3. CLI command: `python prompt_ci.py test --name summariser --version 1.2.0` — exits with code 1 if regression_rate > 5%
4. CLI command: `python prompt_ci.py promote --name summariser --version 1.2.0` — only runs after test passes
5. CLI command: `python prompt_ci.py rollback --name summariser` — restores previous production version

**Bonus:** Add a `diff` command that shows template differences between two versions using `difflib.unified_diff`.

sections.title: Interview Questions — Prompt Engineering at Scale
sections.content:
**Conceptual:**
Q: Why should prompts be versioned separately from application code?
A: Prompt changes affect model behaviour independently of code changes. A code deployment may be stable while a prompt change degrades output quality. Separate versioning enables targeted rollback, attribution of quality regressions, and independent A/B testing.

**Code-based:**
Q: You change a prompt template and want to ensure it doesn't regress. What does your test pipeline look like?
A: (1) Add new version to registry. (2) Render template with each golden case's variables. (3) Call LLM (or mock). (4) Check required keywords present, forbidden absent, min length met. (5) Gate promotion if failure rate > 5%. (6) Run in CI before merging.

**Senior scenario:**
Q: Your team deploys a new prompt that improves average quality score but your P99 latency jumps from 2 s to 12 s. What happened and how do you fix it?
A: The new prompt likely added verbose instructions that significantly increased output token count, inflating generation time for long-tail requests. Fix: (1) Add `max_tokens` cap in the prompt config. (2) Add length distribution to regression suite. (3) Roll back to previous version while investigating. (4) Rewrite prompt to be more concise while preserving quality.

**Wrong answer to avoid:**
Q: How do you decide when an A/B test winner is statistically significant?
Bad: 'When treatment looks better than control in the dashboard' — visual inspection ignores sample size. Correct: require |z| > 1.96 (p < 0.05) and n ≥ 30 per bucket.

questions.q: Which versioning component should be bumped when a prompt change breaks the expected output structure?
questions.explanation: Structural breaking changes increment the major version, signalling downstream consumers that parsing/processing logic may need updating.

questions.q: A deterministic A/B bucket assignment uses `hash(user_id + experiment_id) % 100`. Why include `experiment_id` in the hash?
options: To ensure different users get different buckets
To ensure the same user gets different buckets across different experiments
questions.explanation: Without the experiment_id, a user always lands in the same numeric bucket across all experiments, creating correlation between experiment assignments.

questions.q: Your prompt regression suite has 100 golden cases. After a prompt change, 6 fail. Should you deploy?
options: No, failure rate is 6% which exceeds the 5% gate
Yes, if the 6 failures are for edge cases
Only if the failures are on low-traffic inputs
questions.explanation: A 6% regression rate exceeds the 5% deployment gate. Fix the prompt to pass all or reduce failures below threshold before promoting.

questions.q: When running a prompt A/B test, what is the minimum recommended sample size per bucket before declaring a winner?
questions.explanation: n≥30 per bucket is the minimum for the central limit theorem to apply, enabling valid z-test or t-test significance calculations.

questions.q: A prompt optimised for GPT-4 is deployed to Claude 3 Opus without retesting. What risk does this introduce?
options: Silent quality regression because prompt phrasing may not transfer across model families
questions.explanation: Different model families respond differently to the same phrasing, few-shot examples, and instruction styles. Always re-validate prompts when switching models.
